item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed with this. Maybe put in a few lines of recovered notes detailing the "recruitment" process or something. Those poor souls deserve at least some backstory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip deserves more love, it's at +6 right now (With my upvote). I've seen infinately worse skips with better scores. But I third the suggestion to at least describe how the "humans" piloting the planes look like and act. The fact that you aknowledge their existence but then ignore them is a big letdown.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Edit: Edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- Hide

To link to the GOIs mentioned here, I propose the following edits:

Ideally, Foundation and GOC forces should be sufficient to intercept SCP-2326-1 instances outside of densely populated areas.

to

Ideally, Foundation and GOC forces should be sufficient to intercept SCP-2326-1 instances outside of densely populated areas.

and

Link to SCP-2326-2 discovered in 2000 with the recovery of GRU 'P' Division archives.

to

Link to SCP-2326-2 discovered in 2000 with the recovery of GRU 'P' Division archives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there's spelling or grammar issues, point them out. Or even fix them yourself, I don't mind.

The 'weird unredaction' is important in the context of the article - the Soviet Union was collapsing in 1991. It's relevant to show that this attempted false-flag attack occurred prior to that.

Here's the parts where SCP-XXXX-2 is designated, and the source of SCP-XXXX-1 instances is described:

Groups of SCP-2326-1 in flight strength will spontaneously appear over the North Atlantic at 16,000 m. Such appearances are termed 2326-Tango events. Instances will attempt to fly toward Warsaw Pact industrial centers, major military bases, and other strategic locations. It is presumed that instances of SCP-2326-1 will attempt to destroy these cities with their payloads

Site 2326-A was originally a control facility carefully disguised as a radar site. At some point after [REDACTED], it fell into disrepair until it was restored as a Foundation site after the discovery of its association with 2326-Tango events from rediscovered GRU 'P' Division documents. It contains the remains of a previously unknown variation of the Elbrus-3 computer linked to a Dnestr radar array, designated SCP-2326-2, as well as the remains of a GRU 'P' Division research site. Based on partial recovered documentation, the currently non-functioning network is the control mechanism for SCP-2326-1 instances, and currently in the active mode with no way to return the system to an inactive state. Work to restore functionality is ongoing.

As for surviving instances being unable to answer questions, there's an entire interview of them not being able to respond adequately.

I don't understand what issue you're taking with the request for assistance - it's presumably via a dead drop or letter because both organizations typically do not cooperate - it's unofficial and clandestine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, this entire article seems half-baked and rushed. There are typos and spelling/grammar errors scattered throughout the entire thing, the tone SEEMS fine, but it just feels incredibly bland and kind of incomplete.

Also, what's with the weird not-redaction of ONE part of ONE date in the timeline? Why un-redact the month for only that one entry? At least it's a new form of bad redaction, I guess.

Some specific issues
– hide block

The source of SCP-2326-1 instances is currently unknown. Removal of the source of instances of SCP-2326-1 is ongoing.

I can't find ANY reference in this article for this. Where do they come from and how are we removing the source?

Foundation Site 2326-A is sited at a derelict radar station on the outskirts of Tula, Russia. A research group of Foundation researchers should work to restore SCP-2326-2 to working order to remove the threat that 2326-Tango events pose.

You actually never say what 2326-2 is. Much like -3, we can infer it from, literally, the paragraphs they're in, which is at least decent writing, but in this kind of document they should be explicitly spelled out, ESPECIALLY in the containment procedures.

Surviving instances are unable to answer relevant questions.

Prove it. Where's the documentation?

Internal Document 2326-1 Request for Assistance Level 3/2326 Clearance Required

This document seems very, very poorly formatted/written. Once again, props on managing to explain what this is from context, but WE SHOULD NOT REQUIRE CONTEXT for things like what a document on the file is - they should be stated.

Overall, I feel like this is pretty bad - it's a bit bland and uninspired, and on top of that feels wildly incomplete. I don't really think this should even have made it onto the main site in this stage - it feels like it's still in a draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cities is probably a typo. I can fix that.

The idea is that one city getting wiped off the map would cascade into a worldwide nuclear exchange. May have been slightly inspired by the Sum of All Fears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. Novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you have a good start to this SCP although there are few things that stick out to me.

Projections suggest that if one target is destroyed, there is a 68% of the resulting political situation escalating into an XK-class scenario.

How did you get the 68% in this projection? How would one target being bombed start an XK-class scenario? Could you not just write this off as a rouge terrorist cell?

Instances will attempt to fly toward Warsaw Pact industrial centers, major military bases, and other strategic locations. It is presumed that instances of SCP-2326-1 will attempt to destroy these cities with their payloads.

Is this a typo putting "cities" rather locations?

Anyway this does have potential and the idea of an Infinite Amount of Bombing Sprees sends a chill down my spine and reminds me of the year 2077 for some reason. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good, but my main issue is that you mention SCP-2326-3, but the description never goes into detail as to what they are.

I mean, it's obvious that they're the pilots, but the description should still state that, in my opinion. I'd love to read an interview log with one of those guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes sense, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to clarify that the system had two distinct purposes, how about the following edits:

1)

GRU 'P' division creates Operation Silver, a false flag operation designed to attack Warsaw Pact cities…

to

GRU 'P' division creates Operation Silver, a false flag operation designed to attack Warsaw Pact cities with conventional aerial bombing…

2)

██/██/1978: Operation Silver is operational. A secondary function is implemented - via …

to

██/██/1978: Operation Silver is operational. A secondary function is implemented for use in a nuclear second strike: via …

and 3)

06/██/1991: With the collapse of the Soviet Union imminent, hardline GRU 'P' Divison leaders activate the Silver Apparatus…

to

06/██/1991: With the collapse of the Soviet Union imminent, hardline GRU 'P' Divison leaders activate the false flag function of the Silver Apparatus…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just made the edits, check the top thread of this discussion.

item.title: Re: SCP-2326
item.content:encoded:
Just made a couple edits to alleviate the issues pointed out below
-added a paragraph in the description to emphasize glitchiness
-added another short document about Foundation restoration progress to illustrate the halfway glitchy mode.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Working on that bit right now. I'll put it in when I can. Did I miss anything else obvious? I tried to get as much criticism as I could, but it seemed a couple things slipped through the cracks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Both were functions of the same system, but never intended to be used together in any capacity.

I'll second the "needs to be clearer" sentiment- I didn't catch this bit, and in theory it makes me like this a bit more.

I think really your best option might be more directly explaining in some way just what you've mentioned here: that version 1.0 was supposed to make it look like a botched first strike, version 2.0 overclocked it and turned it into a counterattack… and then the damage kicked it into a glitchy halfway-between mode. You might could even kick it up a few notches on the glitchyness- say, maybe the squadrons are a mix of MiG-29's and V-bombers, flip flop between targets, and hold a mix of duds and live nukes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well written overall, but there's articles that use all these bits to greater effect in my opinion.

SCP-2748 works the 'GOC involvement breaking anomaly' aspect better, while SCP-2254 and SCP-2948 already cover the 'Infinite nukes'(it makes more sense there) and 'Spontaneously Reappearing Aircraft' angles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess it's a little unclear - the false-flag bits, and the nuclear bits, are totally separate parts of the same system. The false flag part was to make UK-looking bombers attack a Warsaw Pact city, leading to war. Whether that was a nuclear strike or not depends on how ballsy they're feeling.

Later on, they decided they could extend the system to act as a lethal, unavoidable second strike that would flatten the west in the event the Soviet are caught on the pointy end of a decapitating strike.

Both were functions of the same system, but never intended to be used together in any capacity.

When the GOC wrecked the system, the system breaks down and things start working not as intended.

Now that you mention it it's a little unclear, but I'm not sure how I can alleviate. You're the first one to bring it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess I'm still not understanding then, because the false flag operation is using nuclear weapons, and will therefore incite nuclear war. That's literally a suicidal course of action, one that was avoided by even the most aggressive regimes on both sides during the Cold War. And infinite bomber fleets attacking NATO is still going to result in worldwide devastation.

The GOC doing the stereotypical GOC thing doesn't even really change anything. Whether nukes are being lobbed at Moscow or Washington, the results are going to be the same in the end. I guess that's what's tripping me up about this; it seems to be written with the idea that a one-sided nuclear strike between the US and the USSR was somehow possible and desirable enough that someone thought it was a good idea to pursue. That goes beyond my suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The GOC finds an anomaly.
They try to break it so it doesn't work.
Instead, it's even more dangerous.
They try to repair their mistake, and can't.
They ask the Foundation to fix the mess.

That was my favorite part. Makes me wonder if the GOC has a designated staffer to make those calls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea (although it's implied rather than explicitly stated) is that the false flag strikes would be lesser and intended to incite war. A secondary function was later added to attack the west with an infinite bomber fleet, but when the GOC attempted to disable it, it caused it to act unpredictably - hence infinite nuclear bombers against the Warsaw pact.

The two functions were NEVER intended to be operated together, it's because the GOC broke it did things get out of hand.

Sorry if that was sort of unclear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In terms of nuclear gamesmanship, I don't understand this at all. This acknowledges the existence of Dead Hand, which is a second-strike fail-deadly deterrence mechanism, and states that it's intended to be used in conjunction with it. But those kinds of deterrents are only there as insurance that if the other party takes out your leadership and command structure in a first strike, that you'll still be able to retaliate and effectively end the world. So this is intended to kill everyone.

But why on earth is it being presented as a GRU-P false flag meant to prolong the existence of the Soviet Union? That makes no sense at all. There's no need to trouble with a false flag operation if the end result is going to be a world-destroying nuclear exchange. At that point, what does it matter who started it? That's also ignoring the fact that the Soviet Union is unlikely to still be around if everyone in the world is dead.

Even in the highly unlikely event that GRU-P is so suicidally unwilling to plan ahead for what might happen in the event of a nuclear strike on a world superpower, and they were all like "yes, this will work perfectly," the infinite tactical nukes that follow will certainly finish the job for whatever is left. You've basically removed the interesting implications to be fished out mutual assured destruction and replaced with a straight doomsday device.

item.title: SCP-2326
item.content:encoded: Hey, this one has been lying around for a while. Thanks to the Site 19 chat for helping get this revised and ready for site.
